patches.find:
8b.3 Point 3 — Audit the eclipse stack in layers: ensemble anchor, Mohe timing, Tier 3 falsifier, and the open optics caveat.

Adopted as steel-man: we restructure our eclipse content into the four-layer presentation and publish the Tier 3 magnetic threshold explicitly. Two structural critiques survive adoption — an ensemble “anchor” fit to the data it anchors is calibration, not anchor; and the Tier 3 magnetic Pass band itself does not distinguish dome from globe (the Sq-dynamo mechanism, calibrated on the same Kim & Chang 2018 ensemble, also produces −17 to −21 nT).

(a) Separate the eclipse claim into four layers: (L1) ensemble anchor — WIN-068 (39-eclipse ensemble); (L2) Mohe timing — WIN-011 (1997 Wang et al. gravity-eclipse); (L3a) Tier 3 magnetic falsifier — PRED-095 (August 2026 eclipse); (L3b) Tier 3 gravity — PRED-TIER-2 (recycled WIN-011; dome’s Tier 3 gravity threshold — August 2026 eclipse prediction, see Predictions tab); (L4) open optics caveat — the logged ray-trace gap. (b) Layered audits force explicit statements about what would falsify the claim. A falsifier that never triggers is not a falsifier; spelling out the Tier 3 threshold is due diligence.

(c) Under adoption we publish a four-row stack. L1 (WIN-068): current verdict Std Model Explains — “the Chapman mechanism (90+ years old) predicts the entire ensemble signal without dome parameters; the dome adds no derivation — only a post-hoc scaling formula calibrated against observed eclipse data.” Under adoption this is explicitly labeled a calibration layer, not an anchor. L2 (WIN-011): current verdict Not Demonstrated — the 6–7 µGal Wang signal is within 1997-era LaCoste-Romberg instrumental noise; Van Camp et al. (2001) found no eclipse gravity signal with four superconducting gravimeters (~1,000× more precise). L3a (PRED-095, magnetic): Genuinely prospective (dome registration 2026-03-12 via Git + OpenTimestamps; our tracker entry 2026-04-04; window closes 2026-08-12, verdict pending). Under adoption we publish an explicit magnetic threshold — “Pass = observed −17 to −21 nT at >80% coverage stations, ≥3 independent sites, pre-registered instruments; Fail = outside this range, or signal explained by ionospheric SQ variation alone.” A Pass in this band does not distinguish dome from globe — both predict signals in this range; only the dome claims a novel derivation path, and WIN-068's code_analysis tag (derives_from_dome:false) confirms there is no independent dome derivation to distinguish. The dome's Pass band is moreover calibrated on the same Kim & Chang (2018) 39-eclipse ensemble that L1 already characterizes as Std Model Explains; the numerical specificity of the threshold is a product of shared training data, not an independent dome forecast. The Fail-clause concession that “signal explained by ionospheric SQ variation alone” would falsify the claim is in tension with the Pass criterion: Chapman's mechanism is the SQ-dynamo, so a confirmed signal in this band is consistent with both models simultaneously, and triggering the Fail-clause would require abandoning the Pass-clause's framing of what counts as a signal. If the dome has not pinned the threshold to this precision in its own published materials, we cite the gap as our finding. L3b (PRED-TIER-2, gravity): Recycled — restates WIN-011 with 1997 Wang et al. gravity-eclipse data; the falsification criterion for this layer is independent replication of the gravity signal at the 2026 eclipse sites. L4 (optics): the dome already logs the ray-trace gap; we credit the disclosure. A logged gap is not a solved gap.

(d) Adopted: layered presentation, explicit falsifier threshold. Unchanged: (i) an ensemble anchor fit to the data it “anchors” is the OTS-wrong-side pattern in physics form — the right operation applied to the wrong side; an anchor is what data hits from outside, a fit is what data draws toward itself (see the Timestamp Error tab); (ii) L3a's Pass band is the Chapman (1933) Sq-dynamo prediction calibrated on the same Kim & Chang (2018) ensemble that anchors L1 — a Pass does not distinguish dome from globe, and there is no independent dome derivation (WIN-068 derives_from_dome:false) that would let the Pass criterion arbitrate between the two models.

ADOPTED (FULL as steel-man): four-layer eclipse stack as audit format; ensemble-anchor and L3a non-discrimination critiques unchanged.
patches.replace:
8b.3 Point 3 — Audit the eclipse stack in layers: ensemble anchor, Mohe timing, Tier 3 falsifier, and the open optics caveat.

Adopted as steel-man: we restructure our eclipse content into the four-layer presentation and publish the Tier 3 magnetic threshold explicitly. Two structural critiques survive adoption — an ensemble “anchor” fit to the data it anchors is calibration, not anchor; and the Tier 3 magnetic Pass band itself does not distinguish dome from globe, because calibrated Chapman-Sq-dynamo fits on the same class of 20th-century eclipse-magnetic observations predict the same −17 to −21 nT range.

(a) Separate the eclipse claim into four layers: (L1) ensemble anchor — WIN-068 (39-eclipse ensemble); (L2) Mohe timing — WIN-011 (1997 Wang et al. gravity-eclipse); (L3a) Tier 3 magnetic falsifier — PRED-095 (August 2026 eclipse); (L3b) Tier 3 gravity — PRED-TIER-2 (dome tier-system identifier for the 2026 eclipse gravity prediction, recycled from WIN-011; see Predictions tab); (L4) open optics caveat — the logged ray-trace gap. (b) Layered audits force explicit statements about what would falsify the claim. A falsifier that never triggers is not a falsifier; spelling out the Tier 3 threshold is due diligence.

(c) Under adoption we publish a four-row stack. L1 (WIN-068): current verdict Std Model Explains — “the Chapman mechanism (90+ years old) predicts the entire ensemble signal without dome parameters; the dome adds no derivation — only a post-hoc scaling formula calibrated against observed eclipse data.” Under adoption this is explicitly labeled a calibration layer, not an anchor. L2 (WIN-011): current verdict Not Demonstrated — the 6–7 µGal Wang signal is within 1997-era LaCoste-Romberg instrumental noise; Van Camp et al. (2001) found no eclipse gravity signal with four superconducting gravimeters (~1,000× more precise). L3a (PRED-095, magnetic): Genuinely prospective (dome registration 2026-03-12 via Git + OpenTimestamps; our tracker entry 2026-04-04; window closes 2026-08-12, verdict pending). Under adoption we publish an explicit magnetic threshold — “Pass = observed −17 to −21 nT at >80% coverage stations, ≥3 independent sites, pre-registered instruments; Fail = outside this range, or signal explained by ionospheric SQ variation alone.” The dome actually publishes a dual-baseline prediction structure — BOU 2017 (per-station ∼−10 nT) and W004 empirical (per-station ∼−20 nT) — and internal code adds multipliers (KAPPA, latitude factor) producing a third value near −29 nT; our non-discrimination critique targets the W004 cluster (−17 to −21 nT, which calibrated Chapman-Sq-dynamo fits also predict), while the BOU-cluster band and the monitor.py −29 nT value are treated in Section 4.2 (dual-baseline analysis) and the WIN-025 globe-fingerprint. A Pass in this band does not distinguish dome from globe — both predict signals in this range; only the dome claims a novel derivation path, and WIN-068’s code_analysis tag (derives_from_dome:false) confirms there is no independent dome derivation to distinguish. The dome’s Pass band is moreover fitted to the same body of observed eclipse-magnetic magnitudes that L1 characterizes (Kim & Chang 2018 39-eclipse ensemble, and the BOU 2017 single-event baseline the dome explicitly cites); the numerical specificity of the threshold is a product of shared training data, not an independent dome forecast. Note also that the dome’s own internal monitor.py produces a ∼−29 nT prediction for the August 2026 eclipse (delta_Z = −18.22 × coverage × KAPPA 1.672, i.e., KAPPA applied on top of a BOU baseline into which KAPPA is already absorbed), which falls outside the published −17 to −21 nT Pass band — either the Pass band was set after seeing the W004-cluster numbers (goalpost-setting after data), or the dome has internal prediction heterogeneity the Pass band suppresses. See the WIN-025 globe-fingerprint for the three-formula audit. The Fail-clause concession that “signal explained by ionospheric SQ variation alone” would falsify the claim is in tension with the Pass criterion: Chapman’s mechanism is the SQ-dynamo, so a confirmed signal in this band is consistent with both models simultaneously, and triggering the Fail-clause would require abandoning the Pass-clause’s framing of what counts as a signal. If the dome has not pinned the threshold to this precision in its own published materials, we cite the gap as our finding. The dome also publishes four nominally model-discriminating sub-tests (E-PRED-A peak-tracks-geometry, E-PRED-B Hartland-beats-Ebro, E-PRED-C SG gravimeters 0.0 µGal, E-PRED-D non-path stations below noise floor) for the August 2026 eclipse — these are evaluated in Section 4.2 Eclipse and in Section 9; their status does not change the non-discrimination assessment of the published magnitude-only Pass band above. L3b (PRED-TIER-2, gravity): Recycled — restates WIN-011 with 1997 Wang et al. gravity-eclipse data; the falsification criterion for this layer is independent replication of the gravity signal at the 2026 eclipse sites. L4 (optics): the dome already logs the ray-trace gap; we credit the disclosure. A logged gap is not a solved gap.

(d) Adopted: layered presentation, explicit falsifier threshold. Unchanged: (i) an ensemble anchor fit to the data it “anchors” is the OTS-wrong-side pattern in physics form — the right operation applied to the wrong side; an anchor is what data hits from outside, a fit is what data draws toward itself (see the Timestamp Error tab); (ii) L3a’s Pass band is the Chapman (1933) Sq-dynamo prediction fitted to the same body of observed eclipse-magnetic magnitudes that anchors L1 (Kim & Chang 2018 ensemble and the BOU 2017 baseline the dome explicitly cites) — a Pass does not distinguish dome from globe, and there is no independent dome derivation (WIN-068 derives_from_dome:false) that would let the Pass criterion arbitrate between the two models.

ADOPTED (FULL as steel-man): four-layer eclipse stack as audit format; ensemble-anchor and L3a non-discrimination critiques unchanged.
patches.rationale: EXP-195: Replace entire p8b-3-eclipse-layers block addressing dual-baseline acknowledgment (ISS-1233), E-PRED cross-reference (ISS-1234), Kim&Chang attribution softening (ISS-1235), monitor.py -29nT inconsistency (ISS-1236)

patches.replace:
rather than any dome parameter.

The second derivation is the same formula in disguise. The dome publishes both the wins.html shorthand f = c/(2 × disc_thickness) and, on its live-power page, a second form f = v_a / (2 × r_disc) with v_a = 1.574c. A fair-minded defender will point to that second form as the "real" dome-parameter derivation, since both v_a and r_disc sit in the dome's constants box. The defense fails on the numbers. The aetheric wave speed v_a = 1.574c is numerically ≈ πc/2 = 1.5708c (ratio 1.0020), the standard phase velocity of the fundamental mode of a spherical cavity resonator of Earth's radius — a textbook ionospheric-waveguide result, not a dome prediction. The disc radius r_disc = 20,015 km equals π × R_earth = 20,015.09 km to four decimals — Earth's half-circumference under the polar azimuthal-equidistant projection used by every globe-to-flat mapping since Snyder (1987). Substitute both into f = v_a / (2 × r_disc) and the live-page formula collapses to (πc/2)/(2 × πR_earth) = c/(4 R_earth) = c/(2 D_earth) — the wins.html formula in different variable names. The closing tell is the ratio itself: v_a/c = 1.574 = 20,015 / 12,717 = r_disc / disc_thickness. The dome's two "different" derivations are the same derivation; v_a was chosen precisely so the live-page numbers reproduce the wins.html numbers. The live-power form does not rescue the claim — it re-dresses the same Earth-sphere arithmetic.

Same circuit, two incompatible wave speeds. The dome's live-power page asserts that WIN-001 (Tesla 11.78 Hz) and WIN-029 (Schumann 7.83 Hz) sit on the same aetheric circuit, with cross-correlation r = 0.999 between Tesla's frequency and the Schumann fundamental. A single medium, however, has a single propagation speed. The two derivations do not: WIN-001 uses v_a = 1.574c in f = v_a / (2 × r_disc), while WIN-029 uses vacuum c in H_firmament = c / (4f), yielding H = 299,792 / (4 × 7.83) ≈ 9,572 km (claimed 5.3% match to the model's H = 9,086 km). Apply v_a consistently across the "same circuit" and WIN-029's ceiling shifts to v_a / (4 × 7.83) ≈ 15,066 km — 66% higher than the dome's published H, destroying WIN-029's match to the firmament apex. Apply c consistently to WIN-001 via f = c / (2 × r_disc) and the Tesla frequency drops to c / (2 × 20,015) = 7.49 Hz — far from 11.78 Hz. The dome preserves both WINs only by selecting c for one and v_a for the other, on the same circuit, without reconciliation. This is the diagnostic signature of per-WIN parameter fitting, not unified physical modeling.

Tesla's location destroys the dome interpretation.
patches.rationale: EXP-196: Insert two paragraphs in WIN-001 — v_a/(2xr_disc) formula shown to be same identity (ISS-1250); cross-WIN c-vs-v_a speed swap destroys WIN-029 match (ISS-1251)
